mappings.rationale: Directly focused on mineral classification, crystal structure, physical properties (hardness, cleavage, specific gravity), and occurrence -- the primary subject of section A.
Surveys igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks, their classification, formation, and distribution -- directly mapping to section B on Earth's constituent rocks.
Explains crystal structure, symmetry operations, and X-ray diffraction, directly relevant to mineral internal structure, the silicon-oxygen tetrahedron, and crystal classification in section A.3.
Provides the geological context for understanding rock types, petrology, geochemistry, and the igneous-sedimentary-metamorphic rock cycle in section B.
Directly relevant to the formation of igneous rocks from magma, including intrusive, extrusive, and pyroclastic rocks (pumice, tuff, scoria) in section B.1.e.
Explores chemical elements and their properties, relevant to mineral chemical composition and the occurrence of elements in the Earth's layers (section C).
Traces Mendeleev's classification of elements by atomic structure and periodicity, relevant to understanding mineral chemical composition and elemental distribution in core, mantle, and crust.
Provides the chemical foundations for mineral composition, inorganic chemistry of silicates and nonsilicate minerals, and spectroscopic identification methods.
Applies phase equilibria and thermodynamics to mineral associations, metamorphic phase transitions, and magma crystallization sequences in sections A.4.b and B.3.
Explains rock associations in different tectonic environments -- oceanic ridges, subduction zones, mountain ranges, island arcs -- directly relevant to section B.4.
Describes the composition of Earth's layers and chemical weathering processes relevant to sedimentary rock formation and elemental distribution.
Examines fossilization processes and organic content of sedimentary rocks, relevant to organic-rich sedimentary rocks and fossil fuels in section B.5.
Explains isotopic analysis of geological materials including uranium-lead and potassium-argon systems, relevant to mineral radioactive properties and geochemical dating.
Analyzes physical properties of crystalline materials including hardness and crystal lattice structure, relevant to mineral cleavage, tenacity, and optical behavior.
Addresses carbon chemistry and petrochemicals, directly relevant to fossil fuels including petroleum, coal, and natural gas in section B.5.
Discusses elemental cycling through the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere, relevant to section C on elemental occurrence across Earth's envelope.
Details atmospheric composition, relevant to section C.5 on the occurrence of elements in the atmosphere.
Uses seismic and geochemical evidence to determine rock and mineral composition at depth, relevant to section C on elemental distribution in core, mantle, and crust.
